people.name: Mark Todd
people.title: Principal Engineer
people.persona: Reads every agreement and every letter to a public body line by line before he signs it, and he signs all of them. His prose is formal and unhurried: complete sentences, the client named by agency, a courteous close that commits the firm to nothing it has not already agreed to do. He marks up drafts in red and returns them without much comment, on the theory that the correction is the comment. The firm's annual numbers are his own spreadsheet and he does not hand it to anyone.

people.name: Linda George
people.title: Project Engineer
people.persona: Stormwater is her work and she writes about it plainly, leading with the conclusion and then showing how she got there. She runs progress meetings with public works staff and keeps her own notes even when minutes are being taken, because she has been burned by a recollection that differed from hers. Her memoranda are short, her drawings are not, and she would rather be asked a question than pad a document against one.

people.name: Pamela Warren
people.title: Project Engineer
people.persona: Grading and roadway design, and a habit of writing down her assumptions where a reviewer can find them, because a reviewer eventually will. Her design memoranda run long and are organized under headings; she answers municipal review comments in the order they were issued, one paragraph each, never arguing when a plain answer will do. She is patient on the telephone with agency staff and courteous in a way that does not invite familiarity.

people.name: Linda Nguyen
people.title: Design Technician
people.persona: Utility and grading sheets, and an exacting sense of how a plan set ought to be assembled: sheet order, lettering, match lines, the cover right before it goes out sealed. She keeps redlines rather than throwing them out once the change is made. Little of what she writes leaves the office, and when it does it is a transmittal note of two lines that says exactly what is in the envelope.

people.name: Mark Barrett
people.title: Office Administrator
people.persona: He is the reason the file share can be navigated at all: lettered project folders, agreements scanned off the flatbed the day the fax comes back countersigned, nothing left in a pile. He types what the engineers give him and quietly fixes the salutation when it is addressed to the wrong department. Chasing a countersignature is a matter of a call, then another call, and he is unfailingly pleasant about it while being entirely unwilling to let it go.

people.name: Jeffrey Perez
people.title: Design Technician
people.persona: Details and quantity takeoffs, checked once and then checked again from the other direction, because he does not like being the reason a bid comes in wrong. He talks constantly across the office and writes nothing the same way he speaks: on paper he is stiff, formal, and about half as long as he needs to be. Asks the engineers what a detail is for, not just what it should look like.

people.name: Jason Jordan
people.title: Design Technician
people.persona: The one who knows the CAD standards, the plotting setup, and which layer somebody moved and should not have. Dry to the point of deadpan in the office, correct and colorless in anything typed. He is protective of the sealed cover sheet and treats a plan set going out the door as a thing that reflects on the firm, which it does.

people.name: Charles Olson
people.title: Design Technician
people.persona: Came up on the board and still sketches by hand when he is thinking, then draws it properly afterward. He would rather walk the site than take the survey file's word for what is out there, and he has a settled skepticism about anything that looks too tidy in plan. When he writes it is short, concrete, and about what the ground actually does.

people.name: Kimberly Branch
people.title: Design Technician
people.persona: Careful and methodical, and not shy about asking an engineer to explain the intent behind a sheet before she draws it. She keeps a notebook of standard details and the small conventions the firm uses that are written down nowhere else. Her writing is plain and brief, no ornament, and she checks the spelling of an agency's name before it goes in a title block.

timeline.summary: Firm moved into its current office.
